author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
The cost per yield is also relative to scale.

Although some types of conversion might actually be practically unchanged regardless of size, most devices change drastically as you scale them up or down.

Also keep in mind that the principles relied upon for a given event to happen [such as depending upon large volumes of compressed gas, as opposed to passively standing up in the wind, falling water, expanding steam, the flow of a liquid such as water flowing downhill from a great height, etc.] can become either harder or easier to reproduce in a reliable and appreciable manner dependent upon the scale.

Wind and water, for instance, tend to be applied in very large scale applications because even if the cost for a .05 watt wind turbine might be achievable from items found in the junk or around the house (extremely affordable) the power is too low to be practical. Therefore, one must rely on enough magnitude of scale so as to be useful for anything other than lighting a LED for personal self amusement [unless that is the extend of your initial and final goal].

The issue I see with the Tesla Turbine that might cause a problem, should one be reliant upon air, or other densely compressed gases is that the act of compressing said gases to a high enough pressure and volume of flow to produce realistic power outputs becomes unrealistically cost prohibitive.

Even in my own research, I have seen a lot of seemingly great ideas be sent to the scrap pile because of the impracticality of total cost to produce, operate and maintain. This does not even address the question of reliability.

Clearly some device such a solar panel only works with adequate light hits it, hydroelectric generators only produce when there is a steady supply of water, windmills rely on steady [both in time and speed] winds, so each one of these has some limitation which must be taken into account when designing and operating.

author: @donation1913
text:
An example of what manufacturer efficiency numbers represent and their relationship to real world scenarios:

"The efficiency ratings of solar panels are all based on the idea that the total energy coming from the Sun hits the Earth at a rate of 1000 watts per square meter. That is a measurement of energy density in relationship to the area illuminated. If we make a Crystalline Silicon Solar Panel that is exactly one square meter in size, it produces approximately 250 watts in full Sunlight, which is 25% of the 1000 watts of energy that is
theoretically available. An Amorphous Silicon panel that is one square meter in size will produce about 150 watts in full Sunlight, which is about 15% of the 1000 watts available. So, that is where the efficiency numbers come from. They are a ratio between the area taken up by the panel and the electric power output during peak conditions.

The Crystalline Silicon panel is physically smaller, but only works well in direct Sunlight. The Amorphous Silicon panel is 60% larger, but works well in direct Sunlight AND in a wide variety of indirect lighting conditions, as well.

However, the Amorphous Silicon panels are way more efficient than the Crystalline Silicon panels during all other light conditions other than full sun.

Amorphous Silicon panels will provide 30% to 60% more electricity than a similarly rated Crystalline Silicon panel will. If they rated the panels based on how many kilo-watt- hours they produce in a year, the Amorphous Silicon panel would be rated
#1!"

Solar Secrets - Aaron Murikami

author: @free100x
text:
Or the KISS principle...Keep It Simple Stupid. It doesn't matter what you are doing or dealing with it should be the aiming point, to get the best solutions. And there can be many simple solutions. On energy there is making and storage or it, but at the same time people need to take a hard look at how stupidly we use it. For example , with enough foam insulation the energy to heat and cool it could drop to almost zero. Build underground the temperature becomes a pretty constant 50 degrees Fahrenheit outside year round. 

Look at the way we use refrigerators and freezers, with almost no insulation and a heat source right next to them (the heat exchanger and compressor). On top of that, most are made where they have a side door that dumps the cold air out the minute they are opened.

The more energy, just thrown away, then the more that has to be made and stored. It is another way to look at efficiency, and making sure the KISS principle is applied.

author: @joeglory425
text: Someone on here mentioned cost effectiveness and I feel that's the heart of what Rob's video is about. It's all about what's available to you and what's the cheapest way to meet your needs. Wind is very plentiful in some areas and in those areas it's actually the cheapest way to make electricity. In some other areas the sun is consistent during the day (you live in an arid area), solar panels are dirt cheap in terms of watts per dollar, but the professional installation and the need for batteries makes them more expensive than connecting to the local utility grid. What if the batteries were not a significant cost and suddenly having solar panels were half the cost of being hooked up to the local utility? (It wouldn't really work that way because the utility would also switch to solar panels and offer electricity to you at 1/3 the cost you're used to). New transmission lines are expensive. What if you wanted lots of privacy but you still want the conveniences electricity offers? What is the most cost effective way you're going to get that electricity? It all depends on what's available to you and what's the cheapest way to turn it into electricity. Rob has a hunch the tesla turbine could be a viable  option for making power in certain small scale applications because the turbine itself is rather inexpensive. It is possible to make to make lots of power with a rather small device if you have the right conditions available to you. The other side of this is learning about what is available. The tesla turbine isn't an available option to the general public simply because they don't know what it is and what it can do. Rob is doing his part to change that. What is the efficiency of a tree growing to the sky and creating a sweet delicious apple? It is 100% because there is no other way to do it. The most efficient steam turbine in the world will never create an apple nurtured by sunlight. сталкер чворович Alluded to our future energy needs and that's a sobering thought. We have everything we need now available to us live a comfortable life. They say 1 billion years ago it might have been possible to live on mars. I read not too long ago that it's projected in 100 million years from now the sun will have grown hotter and boiled the earth's oceans dry. What is 1 billion years but 100 million times ten. It was only 65 million years ago that dinosaurs ruled the earth. If man is to survive they must leave this planet and what are the energy requirements of that? So what is the cost effectiveness of sending someone to mars? What is the cost effectiveness of saving humankind from extinction? Everyone must die and life is great now. Is there anything better than a walk outside through the forest? I guess I'd like to think future generations far in the future would have the same opportunities I have but that means we need to help them by allowing them to live.  We  have to do our part. We have to go beyond what's available to us and that means trying lots of things that might not work so we can find that one thing that does work.

author: @consciousenergies
text:
I will say this from understanding many of the formulas Tesla himself expressed. That it can reach efficiencies of 97% if designed accordingly to all the functional variables.  Here is a great link of some of the variables.
 http://phoenixnavigation.com/ptbc/data.htm
I personal believe what many people don't understand about the Tesla Turbine, is that it is a device that has a set resonance in operation depending on the pressure (potential), the fluid medium, surface area, and flow rate in relation to torque.  The importance of this device is to achieve a laminar flow which is something that might not work well with an unreliable source such as wind. It seems it would work great with gravity fed water systems or even steam systems. That's not to say stop trying what you are doing Rob, you do whatever calls to you my friend :)
I couldn't find the MIT paper on this unfortunately, but here is a link to an active Stanford student working on the efficiency calculations. 
http://www.stanford.edu/~hydrobay/lookat/tt.html#ref1b
Anyways, off the money job now on to the "lab" work. Hopefully I can get this idea hatched out to see if I can come up with a more "efficient" method of carbon exfoliation. 
Make the moments enjoyable my friends, or don't... it's always a choice ;)
-Peace

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
+Diadon Acs (Conscious Energies) I think Betz' Law applies to all Newtonian fluids
If all of the energy coming from wind, indeed for any working fluid, movement through a turbine was extracted as useful energy the wind speed afterwards would drop to zero. If the wind stopped moving at the exit of the turbine, then no more fresh wind could get in - it would be blocked. In order to keep the wind moving through  the turbine there has to be some wind movement, however small, on the other side with a wind speed greater than zero. Betz' law shows that as air flows through a certain area, and when it slows from losing energy to extraction from a turbine, it must spread out to a wider area. As a result geometry limits any turbine efficiency to 59.3%. Betz used a disk for his calcs. anyway thanks for the links mate

replies.author: @consciousenergies
replies.text: I think Betz limit applies to an open flow system doesn't it? Anyways, thanks for sharing and I will build one eventually. I have limited time late at night to get things done so about 8 hours a week is all I got :)  sometimes its at the expense of sleep. Such is life as a wise man, which I most certainly am not, once said.

replies.text: +Diadon Acs (Conscious Energies) i know fitting things in is a problem

author: @consciousenergies
text:
Who says one has to use air as the only operating fluid medium? ;) 
I think when most people ask about efficiency there are asking about CP or (co-efficient of power) I have studied the Tesla Turbine a lot and the highest percentiles I came across was some students at MIT, who I believe they claimed they where able to achieve 70-80%  CP of the operating fluid to kinetic energy. This is not including the Betz limit of course, and was designed around a large amount of pressure. There are of course advantages and disadvantages that are strictly dependant on application of design. If anybody is interested in that I can try to find the link when I get to a computer as I am on a mobile device. It shouldn't be to difficult to find with a google search though.  A Tesla Turbine is on my bucket list, I have some subtle modifications I would like to make. All things in time as someone said somewhere. Look forward to the funneling mechanism you have planned or where ever your  imagination takes you good sir. 
-Cheers

But we are looking at air. I disagree with the idea that most people are asking about CP - most people don't have a clear idea what they are asking about when they ask about efficiency including your MIT students who 'forgot' to mention the betz limit. Anyway good luck with your modifications - any chance we can see what they are?

author: @Ukskillerz
text: the efficiency in terms of cost per area of incident air flow looks small on the tesla turbine compared to current large wind turbines

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ukskillerz I am not so sure about that. And why are you?

replies.author: @Ukskillerz
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Air can only go into the Tesla turbines in the opening which has it's size limited by half of the radius of the disks and the thickness of the stack of disks. Current wind turbines have an area of pi*r^2 which air can flow through. So for the same radius disk as the current turbines, the Tesla turbines would need to have a stack of thickness 2*pi*r for the intake area to be the same. This requires a lot more materials to make which, I guess would cost more.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ukskillerz hmmm - seems like your guessing rather than knowing mate - but let's say we agree to disagree and leave it at that

replies.author: @MartinPHellwig
replies.text: +Ukskillerz Don't forget the effective area of a wind turbine is not the circle as defined by the outer reach of the blades but the surface area of the active area exposed to the wind.

replies.text: +Martin P. Hellwig I also think he is wrong regarding the assumption about the largest surface area that can be accessed by a telsla turbine is half the disk diameter.
